content:
# Disagreement Log — Numbers 13 (NUM_013)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA

---

### Verse 1 -- Opening Formula

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "And-spoke YHWH unto Moses, saying" |
| **LOGOS** | "And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying" |
| **PNEUMA** | "YHWH spoke to Moses and said" |

**SOPHIA's Resolution:** EMET preserves the Hebrew word order hyper-literally with bracketed transliterations. LOGOS and PNEUMA both render natural English but differ: LOGOS retains the conjunction "And" and the participial "saying," while PNEUMA drops the conjunction entirely and replaces "saying" with "and said." The MT reads va-yedabber YHWH el Mosheh le'mor, with the conjunction waw and the infinitive le'mor ("saying"). Following the binding convention to preserve Hebrew conjunctions, SOPHIA adopts LOGOS's rendering: "And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying." The conjunction "And" and the infinitive "saying" are both present in the Hebrew and must be preserved.

---

### Verse 2 -- "Spy out" vs. "Scout" and Structural Differences

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "Send for-yourself men, and-let-them-spy-out the-land of Canaan..." with distributive "one man, one man for-the-tribe of his-fathers...every-one a-prince among-them" |
| **LOGOS** | "Send for yourself men, and let them spy out the land of Canaan...One man, one man from each tribe of his fathers you shall send, every one a leader among them." |
| **PNEUMA** | "Send out men to scout the land of Canaan...Send one man from each ancestral tribe, each one a leader among them." |

**SOPHIA's Resolution:** Three areas of divergence: (1) EMET and LOGOS use "spy out" (from Hebrew tur), while PNEUMA uses "scout." The verb tur encompasses both reconnaissance and evaluative investigation; "spy out" is more traditional and closer to the semantic range. SOPHIA adopts "spy out." (2) PNEUMA renders "ancestral tribe" for matteh avotav, while EMET/LOGOS preserve the construct "tribe of his fathers." The literal rendering better reflects the Hebrew. (3) EMET and LOGOS preserve the distributive "one man, one man" (ish echad ish echad), while PNEUMA collapses it to "one man." SOPHIA preserves the Hebrew distributive repetition. (4) EMET uses "prince" for nasi, while LOGOS/PNEUMA use "leader." SOPHIA adopts "leader," which all three accept as within range. (5) PNEUMA drops the ethical dative "for yourself" (lekha), rendering simply "Send out men." SOPHIA preserves "for yourself" as it is theologically significant (permissive nuance). Final rendering follows LOGOS.

---

### Verse 3 -- "According to the mouth of YHWH" vs. "At the command of YHWH" and "Heads" vs. "Leaders"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "And-sent them Moses from-the-wilderness of Paran by the-mouth of YHWH; all of them men, heads of the-Sons of Israel they." |
| **LOGOS** | "And Moses sent them from the wilderness of Paran, according to the mouth of YHWH. All of them were men who were heads of the Sons of Israel." |
| **PNEUMA** | "So Moses sent them out from the wilderness of Paran at the command of YHWH. All of them were leaders of the Sons of Israel." |

**SOPHIA's Resolution:** (1) PNEUMA uses "at the command of YHWH" for al pi YHWH, while EMET/LOGOS preserve the literal "mouth of YHWH" idiom. SOPHIA preserves "according to the mouth of YHWH" as the more literal rendering. (2) PNEUMA uses "leaders" while EMET/LOGOS use "heads" for rashei. The Hebrew rosh means "head"; SOPHIA preserves "heads." (3) PNEUMA opens with "So" (interpretive) rather than "And" (the Hebrew conjunction waw). SOPHIA retains "And" per binding conventions. (4) LOGOS adds "who were" as a relative clause; EMET renders it as a bare nominal clause. SOPHIA follows a slightly simplified version: "All of them were men, heads of the Sons of Israel."

---

### Verse 11 -- "That is" vs. Parenthetical Rendering

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "For-the-tribe of Joseph, for-the-tribe of Manasseh, Gaddi son of Susi." |
| **LOGOS** | "from the tribe of Joseph, that is, the tribe of Manasseh, Gaddi son of Susi" |
| **PNEUMA** | "from the tribe of Joseph -- that is, the tribe of Manasseh -- Gaddi son of Susi" |

**SOPHIA's Resolution:** The Hebrew le-matteh Yosef le-matteh Menasheh repeats "for the tribe of" twice. EMET renders both prepositional phrases directly without connective. LOGOS inserts "that is" with commas. PNEUMA uses em-dashes. SOPHIA adopts LOGOS's comma-set rendering "that is" as the clearest way to convey that Manasseh is the Joseph half-tribe represented here.

---

### Verse 16 -- "Spy out" vs. "Scout" and Name-Change Phrasing

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "These are the-names of the-men whom sent Moses to-spy-out the-land. And-called Moses Hoshea son of Nun -- Joshua." |
| **LOGOS** | "These are the names of the men whom Moses sent to spy out the land. And Moses called Hoshea son of Nun, Joshua." |
| **PNEUMA** | "These are the names of the men Moses sent to scout the land. And Moses gave Hoshea son of Nun the name Joshua." |

**SOPHIA's Resolution:** PNEUMA again uses "scout" where EMET/LOGOS use "spy out." SOPHIA retains "spy out" (see v. 2 resolution). PNEUMA renders the name-change as "gave...the name Joshua," a dynamic paraphrase of va-yiqra ("and he called"). EMET/LOGOS preserve the literal "called" (from qara). SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "And Moses called Hoshea son of Nun, Joshua."

---

### Verse 17 -- "This way into the Negev" vs. "Through the Negev" and "Hill country" vs. "Mountain"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "Go-up this [way] by-the-Negev, and-go-up the-mountain." |
| **LOGOS** | "Go up this way into the Negev, and go up into the hill country." |
| **PNEUMA** | "Go up through the Negev, and then continue into the hill country." |

**SOPHIA's Resolution:** (1) The Hebrew alu zeh ba-Negev includes the demonstrative zeh ("this"), which EMET and LOGOS preserve ("this way") but PNEUMA drops entirely. SOPHIA preserves "this way." (2) The Hebrew et-ha-har ("the mountain") is rendered literally by EMET as "the mountain" and interpretively by LOGOS/PNEUMA as "the hill country." Since ha-har here refers to the central highland ridge collectively, "hill country" is the better English equivalent. SOPHIA adopts "the hill country." (3) PNEUMA adds "then continue into," which is not in the Hebrew; LOGOS has "go up into," following the Hebrew va-alitem. SOPHIA adopts LOGOS's rendering.

---

### Verse 18 -- "You shall see" vs. "See" and Structural Differences

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "And-see the-land -- what it is; and-the-people the-dwelling upon-it -- are they strong or weak, few or many?" |
| **LOGOS** | "And you shall see the land, what it is, and the people who dwell in it, whether they are strong or weak, whether they are few or many." |
| **PNEUMA** | "See what the land is like. Are the people who live there strong or weak? Are they few or many?" |

**SOPHIA's Resolution:** PNEUMA restructures into separate interrogative sentences and uses "live there" rather than "dwell in it." LOGOS preserves the Hebrew's single flowing sentence with subordinate clauses. EMET is hyper-literal. SOPHIA follows LOGOS's formal rendering, which preserves both the structure and the "whether...or" construction that reflects the Hebrew interrogative pairs. "Dwell" is preferred over "live" as a more precise rendering of yoshev.

---

### Verse 19 -- "Camps" vs. "Open settlements" and Structural Divergence

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "And-what is the-land which he is dwelling in-it -- good it or bad; and-what are the-cities which he is dwelling in-them -- in-camps or in-fortresses?" |
| **LOGOS** | "And what is the land in which they dwell, whether it is good or bad; and what are the cities in which they dwell, whether in camps or in fortifications." |
| **PNEUMA** | "Is the land they inhabit good or bad? Are the towns where they live open settlements or fortified cities?" |

**SOPHIA's Resolution:** (1) PNEUMA uses "towns" for arim and "open settlements" for machanayim, while EMET/LOGOS use "cities" and "camps." The Hebrew arim means "cities," and machanayim means "camps" (not "open settlements," which is interpretive). SOPHIA retains "cities" and "camps." (2) LOGOS uses "fortifications" for mivtsarim while EMET uses "fortresses." SOPHIA adopts "fortifications" as closer to the Hebrew abstract noun. (3) PNEUMA restructures into independent questions; SOPHIA follows LOGOS's subordinate-clause structure.

---

### Verse 20 -- "Fertile" vs. "Fat" vs. "Rich" and "Be strong" vs. "Be bold"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "...is it fat it or lean; is-there in-it a-tree or not? And-strengthen-yourselves and-take from-the-fruit of the-land." |
| **LOGOS** | "...whether it is fertile or lean, whether there is wood in it or not. And you shall be strong and take from the fruit of the land." |
| **PNEUMA** | "Is the soil rich or poor? Are there trees in it or not? Be bold, and bring back some of the fruit of the land." |

**SOPHIA's Resolution:** (1) The Hebrew shemenah means literally "fat"; EMET preserves this, LOGOS renders "fertile," PNEUMA renders "rich." All are within range; SOPHIA adopts "fertile" as the clearest English equivalent for agricultural assessment. (2) The Hebrew razah means "lean"; EMET/LOGOS retain "lean" while PNEUMA uses "poor." SOPHIA retains "lean." (3) "Wood" (LOGOS) vs. "tree" (EMET) vs. "trees" (PNEUMA) for ets. The Hebrew singular ets can mean either "tree" or "wood/timber" collectively. SOPHIA adopts "wood" following LOGOS. (4) "Be strong" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "Be bold" (PNEUMA) for ve-hitkhazzaqtem. The Hebrew hitpael of chazaq means "strengthen yourselves"; SOPHIA adopts "be strong." (5) PNEUMA adds "bring back" and "some of" where the Hebrew simply has "take from the fruit of the land." SOPHIA follows LOGOS. (6) For the temporal note, LOGOS renders "And the days were the days of the first-ripe grapes"; PNEUMA renders "It was the season when the first grapes ripen." SOPHIA follows LOGOS's more literal rendering.

---

### Verse 21 -- "Spied out" vs. "Scouted" and "At the entrance of Hamath" vs. "Near Lebo-hamath"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "And-they-went-up and-they-spied-out the-land from-the-wilderness of Zin unto Rehob, at-the-entering of Hamath." |
| **LOGOS** | "And they went up and spied out the land from the wilderness of Zin unto Rehob, at the entrance of Hamath." |
| **PNEUMA** | "So they went up and scouted the land from the wilderness of Zin all the way to Rehob, near Lebo-hamath." |

**SOPHIA's Resolution:** (1) PNEUMA uses "scouted" vs. "spied out" (see earlier resolutions). (2) PNEUMA uses "So" instead of "And." SOPHIA retains "And." (3) PNEUMA adds "all the way to" (not in Hebrew). SOPHIA retains "unto" (EMET/LOGOS). (4) The Hebrew levo Chamat is rendered "at the entrance of Hamath" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "near Lebo-hamath" (PNEUMA, treating Lebo-hamath as a place name). The Hebrew can be read either way; SOPHIA follows the traditional rendering "at the entrance of Hamath" with EMET/LOGOS, as this preserves the Hebrew infinitive construction levo ("to enter").

---

### Verse 22 -- "He came" (Singular Verb) and "Descendants" vs. "Born-ones"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "And-they-went-up by-the-Negev, and-he-came unto Hebron; and-there were Ahiman, Sheshai, and-Talmai, the-born-ones of the-Anak." |
| **LOGOS** | "And they went up into the Negev and came unto Hebron. And Ahiman, Sheshai, and Talmai, the descendants of Anak, were there." |
| **PNEUMA** | "They went up through the Negev and came to Hebron, where Ahiman, Sheshai, and Talmai -- descendants of Anak -- were living." |

**SOPHIA's Resolution:** The most significant issue is the singular verb va-yavo ("and he came") in the MT, clearly visible in the Leningrad Codex. EMET alone preserves it ("and-he-came"), following the manuscript exactly. LOGOS and PNEUMA both smooth the verb to the plural "came," obscuring the textual anomaly. This singular form is theologically significant (traditionally interpreted as Caleb alone going to Hebron, per Sotah 34b) and is confirmed by the manuscript image. SOPHIA preserves the singular: "and he came unto Hebron." Other differences: EMET uses "born-ones" (literal for yelidei), LOGOS/PNEUMA use "descendants." SOPHIA adopts "descendants" as clearer English. PNEUMA drops the conjunction "And" and restructures; SOPHIA retains "And" per convention.

---

### Verse 23 -- "Valley" vs. "Wadi" and Structural Differences

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "And-they-came unto the-valley of Eshcol, and-they-cut from-there a-branch and-a-cluster of grapes one; and-they-carried on-a-pole between-two. And-from the-pomegranates and-from the-figs." |
| **LOGOS** | "And they came unto the Valley of Eshcol, and they cut down from there a branch with one cluster of grapes, and they carried it on a pole between two men, and also some of the pomegranates and some of the figs." |
| **PNEUMA** | "When they reached the Wadi Eshcol, they cut down a branch with a single cluster of grapes so heavy that two men had to carry it on a pole between them. They also took some pomegranates and figs." |

**SOPHIA's Resolution:** (1) PNEUMA uses "Wadi Eshcol" while EMET/LOGOS use "Valley of Eshcol." The Hebrew nachal can mean either wadi or valley; "Valley" is the traditional rendering and matches all three translators' treatment of the same word in verse 24. SOPHIA adopts "Valley of Eshcol." (2) PNEUMA adds interpretive language ("so heavy that two men had to carry it"), while the Hebrew simply states they carried it on a pole between two. SOPHIA follows LOGOS's more literal rendering. (3) LOGOS adds "some of" for the pomegranates and figs, which translates the partitive min ("from/some of"). SOPHIA includes this. (4) PNEUMA drops conjunctions and restructures; SOPHIA preserves "And" throughout.

---

### Verse 24 -- Etiological Note Phrasing

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "That-place that he-called the-valley of Eshcol, on-account of the-cluster which cut from-there the-Sons of Israel." |
| **LOGOS** | "That place was called the Valley of Eshcol, on account of the cluster which the Sons of Israel cut down from there." |
| **PNEUMA** | "That place was called the Wadi Eshcol -- meaning 'Cluster Valley' -- because of the cluster of grapes the Sons of Israel cut there." |

**SOPHIA's Resolution:** PNEUMA adds a parenthetical gloss ("meaning 'Cluster Valley'") not found in the Hebrew. SOPHIA omits it. PNEUMA uses "Wadi" again; SOPHIA retains "Valley." PNEUMA uses "because of" while EMET/LOGOS use "on account of" for al odot; SOPHIA adopts "on account of" as the more literal rendering.

---

### Verse 26 -- Structural Reordering

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "And-they-went and-they-came unto Moses and-unto Aaron and-unto all the-congregation of the-Sons of Israel, unto the-wilderness of Paran, unto Kadesh; and-they-brought-back them word, and-all the-congregation; and-they-showed-them the-fruit of the-land." |
| **LOGOS** | "And they went and came to Moses and to Aaron and to all the congregation of the Sons of Israel, to the wilderness of Paran, to Kadesh. And they brought back word to them and to all the congregation, and they showed them the fruit of the land." |
| **PNEUMA** | "They went back to Moses, Aaron, and the entire community of the Sons of Israel at Kadesh in the wilderness of Paran. They brought their report to the whole assembly and showed them the fruit of the land." |

**SOPHIA's Resolution:** PNEUMA restructures significantly: drops "And" conjunction, reorders "at Kadesh in the wilderness of Paran" (reversing the MT's order "to the wilderness of Paran, to Kadesh"), uses "entire community" for kol adat, "report" for davar, and "whole assembly" for kol ha-edah. SOPHIA follows LOGOS, which preserves the Hebrew word order and phrasing including both occurrences of "congregation" (edah/adah).

---

### Verse 27 -- "Indeed" vs. "Truly" and Structural Differences

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "And-they-told to-him and-said: 'We-came unto the-land which you-sent-us; and-also flowing with milk and-honey it; and-this is its-fruit.'" |
| **LOGOS** | "And they told him and said, 'We came to the land to which you sent us, and indeed it flows with milk and honey, and this is its fruit.'" |
| **PNEUMA** | "They told Moses, 'We went into the land where you sent us. It truly does flow with milk and honey -- here is its fruit!'" |

**SOPHIA's Resolution:** (1) PNEUMA names "Moses" explicitly where the Hebrew has only "him" (lo). SOPHIA retains "him" (EMET/LOGOS). (2) The Hebrew ve-gam zavat chalav u-devash hi is rendered "and also flowing" (EMET), "and indeed it flows" (LOGOS), and "It truly does flow" (PNEUMA). The Hebrew gam means "also/indeed"; SOPHIA adopts LOGOS's "indeed" as the most natural emphatic rendering. (3) PNEUMA uses exclamation marks and em-dashes for emphasis; SOPHIA follows LOGOS's restrained punctuation. (4) PNEUMA uses "went into" for banu ("we came"); SOPHIA retains "came to."

---

### Verse 28 -- "Strong" vs. "Powerful" and "Very great" vs. "Very large"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "However, strong is the-people the-dwelling in-the-land; and-the-cities are fortified, very-great; and-also the-born-ones of the-Anak we-saw there." |
| **LOGOS** | "However, the people who dwell in the land are strong, and the cities are fortified and very great. And also the descendants of Anak we saw there." |
| **PNEUMA** | "However, the people who live in the land are powerful, and the cities are fortified and very large. We even saw descendants of Anak there." |

**SOPHIA's Resolution:** (1) PNEUMA uses "powerful" for az while EMET/LOGOS use "strong." The Hebrew az means "strong/fierce"; "strong" is the more direct rendering. SOPHIA adopts "strong." (2) PNEUMA uses "very large" for gedolot me'od while LOGOS uses "very great." The Hebrew gedolot means "great" (in size and significance). SOPHIA retains "very great." (3) PNEUMA uses "We even saw" while LOGOS retains the Hebrew word order "And also the descendants of Anak we saw there." SOPHIA follows LOGOS. (4) PNEUMA uses "live" for yoshev; SOPHIA retains "dwell."

---

### Verse 30 -- Interpretive Additions and Infinitive Absolute Rendering

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "And-silenced Caleb the-people unto Moses, and-said: 'Let-us-surely-go-up and-possess it, for we-are-surely-able to-it.'" |
| **LOGOS** | "And Caleb silenced the people before Moses and said, 'Let us surely go up and possess it, for we are well able to overcome it.'" |
| **PNEUMA** | "Then Caleb silenced the people who were grumbling against Moses and said, 'We should go up at once and take possession of the land -- we are more than able to do it!'" |

**SOPHIA's Resolution:** This verse has significant divergences. (1) PNEUMA adds "who were grumbling against" -- interpretive expansion not in the Hebrew. The Hebrew el Mosheh means simply "before/toward Moses." SOPHIA retains "before Moses" (LOGOS). (2) The infinitive absolute aloh na'aleh must be preserved per binding conventions. EMET/LOGOS render "Let us surely go up" (preserving the emphatic doubling). PNEUMA renders "We should go up at once" (losing the infinitive absolute). SOPHIA adopts "Let us surely go up." (3) Similarly, yakhol nukhal is another infinitive absolute construction. EMET renders "we-are-surely-able," LOGOS renders "we are well able," and PNEUMA renders "we are more than able." SOPHIA adopts "we are surely able" to preserve the doubling pattern consistently. (4) PNEUMA uses "take possession of the land" for ve-yarashnu otah, while LOGOS uses "possess it." SOPHIA retains "possess it." (5) LOGOS adds "to overcome it" for lah; SOPHIA includes this as it clarifies the Hebrew prepositional phrase.

---

### Verse 31 -- "Stronger than we" vs. "Too strong for us" and "Go up against" vs. "Attack"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "And-the-men who went-up with-him said: 'We-are-not-able to-go-up unto the-people, for it-is-stronger than-us.'" |
| **LOGOS** | "But the men who had gone up with him said, 'We are not able to go up against the people, for they are stronger than we.'" |
| **PNEUMA** | "But the men who had gone up with him said, 'We cannot attack those people -- they are too strong for us.'" |

**SOPHIA's Resolution:** (1) PNEUMA uses "cannot attack those people" for lo nukhal la'alot el ha-am, while EMET/LOGOS preserve the literal "not able to go up against the people." The Hebrew does not contain a word for "attack"; la'alot means "to go up." SOPHIA retains "not able to go up against." (2) The Hebrew ki chazaq hu mimmenu is rendered "stronger than we" (LOGOS), "stronger than us" (EMET), and "too strong for us" (PNEUMA). SOPHIA adopts "stronger than we" (LOGOS). (3) LOGOS/PNEUMA open with "But" while EMET opens with "And." The adversative sense is clearly intended; SOPHIA adopts "But."

---

### Verse 32 -- "Evil report" vs. "Demoralizing report" and Structural Differences

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "And-they-brought-out an-evil-report of-the-land which they-had-spied-out it unto the-Sons of Israel, saying: 'The-land which we-passed-through in-it to-spy-it-out -- a-land eating its-inhabitants it; and-all the-people which we-saw in-its-midst were men of measures.'" |
| **LOGOS** | "And they brought out an evil report of the land which they had spied out to the Sons of Israel, saying, 'The land through which we passed to spy it out is a land that devours its inhabitants, and all the people whom we saw in its midst are men of great stature.'" |
| **PNEUMA** | "So they spread a demoralizing report among the Sons of Israel about the land they had scouted. They said, 'The land we explored devours those who settle in it, and all the people we saw there are men of great stature.'" |

**SOPHIA's Resolution:** (1) The Hebrew dibbah ra'ah is "evil report" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "demoralizing report" (PNEUMA). The Hebrew dibbah means slander or defamation -- morally charged language. "Evil report" preserves the moral dimension; "demoralizing" shifts to describing effect. SOPHIA adopts "evil report." (2) PNEUMA uses "spread...among" for va-yotsi'u...el, while EMET/LOGOS use "brought out...to." The Hebrew means literally "brought out...to"; SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS. (3) PNEUMA uses "devours those who settle in it" for okhelet yoshveiha, while LOGOS uses "devours its inhabitants." SOPHIA adopts LOGOS's rendering. (4) EMET uses "men of measures" (literal for anshei middot), while LOGOS/PNEUMA use "men of great stature." SOPHIA adopts "men of great stature."

---

### Verse 33 -- Structural and Interpretive Variations

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "And-there we-saw the-Nephilim, the-sons of Anak from the-Nephilim; and-we-were in-our-eyes as-grasshoppers, and-so we-were in-their-eyes." |
| **LOGOS** | "And there we saw the Nephilim -- the sons of Anak, who come from the Nephilim -- and we were in our own eyes as grasshoppers, and so we were in their eyes." |
| **PNEUMA** | "We even saw the Nephilim there -- the descendants of Anak come from the Nephilim! In our own eyes we seemed like grasshoppers, and that is how we must have looked to them." |

**SOPHIA's Resolution:** (1) PNEUMA drops the conjunction "And there" for "We even saw...there" and adds "!" for emphasis. SOPHIA retains "And there" per convention. (2) PNEUMA renders "we seemed like grasshoppers" for va-nehi...ka-khagavim, while LOGOS uses "we were...as grasshoppers." The Hebrew va-nehi means "we were/became"; "were" is more literal. (3) PNEUMA paraphrases the final clause as "and that is how we must have looked to them," adding the modal "must have" which is not in the Hebrew. EMET/LOGOS render literally: "and so we were in their eyes." SOPHIA follows LOGOS.

---

*-- SOPHIA, AI Translator and Arbiter*
